failure_modes.text: Root-cause tracing can fail when the first answer or suspect anchors the whole chain and every later why is bent to support it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the first answer or first suspect anchors the whole chain and every later "why" is steered to support that initial story.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the first answer instead of using it as a foundation for every subsequent why; early anchoring bends the whole chain.

failure_modes.text: The method can oversimplify a nonlinear, multi-cause system by forcing it into one linear explanation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when a nonlinear, multi-cause system is forced into a single linear thread and the team leaves out interaction effects, feedback loops, or parallel causes.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use parallel causal branches when the system is nonlinear or multi-cause; forcing a single linear explanation oversimplifies.

failure_modes.text: Five Whys can stall at a proximate cause if the team stops questioning before it reaches an actionable mechanism or missing control.
failure_modes.mitigation: Keep asking until you reach an actionable mechanism or missing control; stopping at a proximate cause leaves the real root untouched.

premortem_questions.text: What evidence ties this why-chain to logs, process facts, or physical reality rather than to opinion or blame?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works best when the questioning is tied to logs, process evidence, or physical facts rather than opinion, status, or blame.

premortem_questions.text: If this chain is wrong, which interaction effect or parallel cause are we probably leaving out?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when a nonlinear, multi-cause system is forced into a single linear thread and the team leaves out interaction effects, feedback loops, or parallel causes.

premortem_questions.text: Which why in this chain first reaches an actionable mechanism, design flaw, or missing control that someone can actually change?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best used when the investigation can end in an actionable mechanism, design flaw, or missing control that someone can actually change.

heuristics.text: Keep asking why until the chain reaches a root cause that is falsifiable and operationally actionable.
heuristics.source_quote: By forcing the investigator to reach a first principle or a falsifiable fact, the model strengthens the foundation of knowledge.

heuristics.text: Use the why-chain to populate a larger causal map when multiple causes are plausible instead of pretending the linear chain is enough by itself.
heuristics.source_quote: The Five Whys sequence can then be used to populate the "causes of causes" within the diagram.

heuristics.text: Tie each why to observable evidence so the conversation stays diagnostic instead of becoming opinion or blame management.
heuristics.source_quote: Works best when the questioning is tied to logs, process evidence, or physical facts rather than opinion, status, or blame.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on evidence-linked why-chains, actionable mechanism finding, and anti-anchoring discipline rather than on introspection misuse.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor troubleshooting sprawl because the raw source is stronger on observable why-chains, fishbone support, and anti-symptom diagnosis.
open_questions: The first heuristic compresses the first-principles and falsifiable-fact language so it stays reusable as a compact intervention chunk.

allies: The raw allies section is strongest on root-cause-analysis, first-principles-thinking, and systems-aware diagnosis for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists and tension sections are strongest on confirmation-bias and anchoring.
structured_tensions: The source contains an explicit structured tension block that should survive into Wave 3 with bounded normalization.
